metadata.notes: Voice rewrite to Wingspan structured format — Opus, March 2026. Research-corrected voyage structure, loot tokens, reputation overflow, score dial permanence. Sources: official Stonemaier rulebook, Dized official rules, BGG FAQ, r/boardgames strategy threads.

key_rulings.text: At the start of each voyage, deal 6 character cards to one player. All other players find and take the same 6 characters from their own decks.
Daytime abilities resolve from lowest rank to highest rank. Dusk abilities (loot collection) resolve from highest rank to lowest rank.
When two or more players play the same ranked character, the player with higher reputation resolves first.
Players keep unused character cards from previous voyages. Before each new voyage, 6 additional cards are dealt, so hands grow over the course of the game.
During dusk, you must take one loot token if any remain. You cannot voluntarily skip collecting loot.
Anchor abilities on characters in your ship resolve at the end of each voyage, after all days are complete.
Characters in every player's graveyard are visible to all players at all times.

At the end of each voyage, increase your score dial by your doubloon total, then discard those doubloons. The score dial total is permanent and cannot be lost. If your score exceeds 99 after the third voyage, set the dial to 99 and use doubloons to represent additional wealth.
Voyage 1 lasts 4 days, Voyage 2 lasts 5 days, and Voyage 3 lasts 6 days. The number of days increases each voyage regardless of player count.

If you gain more reputation than the track allows, gain 1 doubloon for each reputation you would have gained. If you lose more reputation than the track allows, lose 1 doubloon for each reputation you would have lost.
Each space on the reputation track displays a number representing doubloons earned by that player at the beginning of a new voyage, rewarding higher reputation positions.
Night abilities resolve after all loot has been collected during dusk. Night resolution order follows the same rank ordering as daytime.
All character abilities are mandatory. When a character's ability triggers at its designated time, you must resolve it — you cannot choose to skip it.

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Board & Dials
- **1 double-sided island board** (508x406mm) — Shared play surface. **Calm** side for first games, **Storm** side for more aggressive loot effects. Both sides show the same day track and loot spaces. Works for all player counts (1-6).
- **6 score dials** — One per player. Circular dials tracking your doubloon total throughout the game. Rotate the dial window to update.
- **6 graveyard tiles** — One per player. Discarded characters go here face-up after each voyage. Everyone can inspect any graveyard at any time.

#### Cards
- **240 character cards** (40 unique characters x 6 identical sets) — Each player gets a complete deck of 40 characters. Each card shows a rank number (1-40), a name, and abilities with timing icons indicating when they trigger.
- **39 Automa cards** — Solo play only; set aside in multiplayer.

#### Tokens
- **48 loot tokens** (bakelite) — 7 types, drawn randomly from the cloth bag each voyage:
  - **Chest** (gold), **Map** (blue), **Amulet** (green), **Barrel** (brown), **Saber** (red), **Hook** (gray), **Relic** (purple/cursed)
  - Token effects depend on which board side you are playing.
- **7 loot tiles** — Quick-reference tiles showing what each loot token does.
- **6 reputation tokens** — One per player, placed on the reputation track.
- **54 doubloon tokens** — Physical coins used alongside score dials for tracking wealth.
- **4 Automa loot tiles** — Solo play only.

#### Other
- **1 midshipman token** — Marks the first player.
- **1 cloth loot bag** — For drawing loot tokens randomly each voyage.
- **1 rulebook + 1 solo rulebook**

subtopics.title: Game Structure & Setup
subtopics.content:
#### How the Game Works
- **3 voyages** of increasing length: Voyage 1 = **4 days**, Voyage 2 = **5 days**, Voyage 3 = **6 days**.
- **Each day** — Everyone simultaneously picks a character card, reveals, then resolves abilities and collects loot based on rank order.
- **Winning** — After all 3 voyages, most **doubloons** wins.
- **Core twist** — Every player starts with the **exact same characters**. The game is about outguessing opponents on which character to play and when.

#### Setting Up
1. **Place the island board** — Use the **Calm side** for first games (flip to **Storm** when comfortable).
2. **Each player grabs:** 1 set of 40 character cards (sorted by number), 1 score dial (set to 0), 1 graveyard tile, 1 reputation token.
3. **Place all reputation tokens** on the center space of the reputation track.
4. **Fill the loot bag** — Drop all 48 loot tokens into the cloth bag.
5. **Deal starting characters** — Randomly draw 6 character cards from one player's deck. **Every other player finds those same 6** in their own deck. All begin with an identical 6-card hand.
6. **Prepare Voyage 1 loot** — 4 days = 4 rows. For each row, draw loot tokens from the bag equal to the number of players. Place face-up in the matching row.
7. **Give the midshipman token** to a random player (first player).

#### Checkpoint
- Each player has: the same 6 character cards in hand, score dial at 0, graveyard tile, reputation token on the track.
- Island board (Calm side for first game) displays 4 rows of face-up loot tokens (one row per day, tokens equal to player count per row).

subtopics.title: The Day Phases
subtopics.content:
#### Each Day Has 5 Phases

#### Phase 1: Sunrise — Pick Your Character
- Everyone simultaneously chooses **1 character card** from hand, places it **face-down**.
- All players flip face-up at the same time.
- **Same rank tie** — Player with **higher reputation** resolves first among tied characters.

#### Phase 2: Daytime — Day Abilities (Low to High)
- Characters with a **sun icon** resolve abilities from **lowest rank to highest**.
- Low-rank characters strike first (daytime abilities tend to be aggressive: stealing, discarding, disrupting).
- **All abilities are mandatory** — You cannot skip even if it hurts you.
- If a character is discarded/removed before its ability triggers, that ability does **not** activate.

#### Phase 3: Dusk — Collect Loot (High to Low)
- Starting with **highest rank**, each player picks **exactly 1 loot token** from that day's row.
- High-rank characters get first pick of the best loot.
- **Loot collection is mandatory** — You must take a token if any remain.

#### Phase 4: Night — Night Abilities Resolve
- Characters with a **moon icon** resolve after all loot is collected.
- Same **low-to-high rank order** as daytime.
- Night abilities often interact with loot you just collected or your board position.

#### Phase 5: Rest
- All played character cards move to your **ship** (area in front of you).
- Characters in your ship may have ongoing effects or **anchor abilities** that trigger at end of voyage.
- Move on to the next day.

subtopics.title: Voyages & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### End of a Voyage
1. **Anchor abilities** — Characters in your ship and loot tokens with an anchor icon resolve end-of-voyage effects. You choose the order for your own abilities.
2. **Score dial update** — Add current doubloon total to your **score dial**, then discard physical doubloon tokens. **The score dial total is permanent** — no game effect can remove locked-in doubloons.
3. **Discard loot** — Return all loot tokens to the bag (unless a specific ability says to keep something).
4. **Ship to graveyard** — Move all characters from ship to graveyard tile (face-up, visible to everyone). **Keep all remaining hand cards** — they carry over.
5. **Reputation income** — At the start of the next voyage, earn doubloons based on reputation track position. Higher position = more income.
6. **Deal new characters** — Draw 6 new characters (same process: randomly select 6, everyone matches).
7. **Prepare loot** — Draw loot tokens for the new voyage (5 rows for Voyage 2, 6 rows for Voyage 3), tokens equal to player count per row.

#### Loot Token Types (Calm Side)
- **Chest** — Worth **5 doubloons** at end of voyage. Best straightforward loot.
- **Map** — Set collection: **7 doubloons per pair**, **12 doubloons for a set of 3**. Accumulating maps across voyages is very powerful.
- **Amulet** — Worth **3 doubloons** at end of voyage.
- **Barrel** — Gain **1 reputation** at end of voyage, OR gain **1 doubloon**.
- **Hook** — Choose: **keep a character in your ship** for the next voyage (skips graveyard), OR gain **2 doubloons**. Strong for preserving anchor abilities.
- **Saber** — **Discard another player's character** from the island. Aggressive and situational.
- **Relic** — **Lose 3 doubloons** at end of voyage. Cursed loot nobody wants, but some characters benefit from relics.

- The **Storm side** changes these effects to be more aggressive and unpredictable.

#### Growing Hands
- **Voyage 1** — Start with 6 cards, play 4, keep 2.
- **Voyage 2** — Receive 6 new cards added to remaining hand (start with ~8 cards).
- **Voyage 3** — Receive 6 more (start with ~9+ cards).
- Options expand each voyage, but so does the information opponents have about your holdings.

#### After Voyage 3
- Game ends after Voyage 3's anchor abilities and final score dial update.
- **Most doubloons on score dial wins** (if score exceeds 99, set dial to 99 and use tokens for extra).
- **Tiebreaker** — Higher reputation wins.

subtopics.title: Reputation & Graveyard
subtopics.content:
#### The Reputation Track
- **6 spaces** on the island board. All player tokens live here.

**Reputation serves three purposes:**
1. **Tie-breaking during play** — When two players play the same rank, higher reputation resolves first (both daytime abilities and dusk loot selection).
2. **Voyage income** — Each space shows a doubloon number. At the start of each new voyage, collect doubloons matching your position. Higher reputation = more income.
3. **Final tiebreaker** — Same doubloon total at game end? Higher reputation wins.

**Overflow rule:**
- At the **top** of the track and gaining more reputation: gain **1 doubloon per overflow reputation**.
- At the **bottom** and losing more reputation: lose **1 doubloon per overflow reputation**.
- Track edges convert reputation into doubloons in either direction.

#### The Graveyard
- Characters move from your ship to your **graveyard** at end of each voyage, placed face-up.
- All graveyards are **public information** — anyone can inspect any graveyard at any time.
- Some characters have **graveyard abilities** that trigger when entering or interacting with other graveyard characters.
- Tracking opponents' graveyards is your best deduction tool.

#### Key Mechanism: Card Memory
- All players receive the same characters each voyage.
- **Track what opponents play** by watching their graveyards.
- After Voyage 1: opponent played 4 characters (visible in graveyard), kept 2 (deducible).
- Cross-reference graveyards with dealt characters to narrow opponents' options.
- By Voyage 3, graveyards are full of information — you will have strong reads on what everyone holds.

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### The Core Tension: Rank vs. Timing
- **High-rank characters** — Pick loot **first** at dusk (best selection) but resolve daytime abilities **last** (most exposed to disruption).
- **Low-rank characters** — Act **first** during daytime (powerful offensive strikes) but get **last pick** of loot.
- **The daily question** — Play high to grab the best loot, or play low to strike first with a strong ability?

#### Read the Loot Before You Pick
- **Excellent loot** (chests, maps for set collection) — Play a **high-rank character** to snag the best piece at dusk.
- **Mediocre loot** (barrels, amulets) or relics (-3 doubloons) — Play a **low-rank character** with a strong daytime ability. You are not missing much at dusk.

#### The Mirroring Problem
- Everyone has the same hand and sees the same loot.
- When loot is great, **everyone** wants to play high — creating a pile-up at the top.
- **Counter-play** — When loot is obviously excellent, consider a medium-rank card. Opponents burn their best characters fighting for first pick while you grab a solid piece without wasting your strongest card.

#### Saving vs. Spending
- Unplayed characters carry forward; played characters go to graveyard.
- **Save strongest characters** for high-value loot days or critical late-voyage moments.
- **Do not hoard everything** — Unplayed characters score 0 doubloons. You must play cards to earn points.
- **Hand management** — Voyage 1: play 4, keep 2. Voyage 2: 5 days. Voyage 3: 6 days.

#### Reputation Matters More Than You Think
- Breaks ties when two players play the same rank.
- Determines loot pick order and ability resolution order in tied situations.
- Push reputation up when you can for consistent advantages.

#### Reputation Is Income
- The reputation track pays doubloon income at the start of each new voyage.
- Over 2 income phases (before Voyages 2 and 3), high reputation compounds significantly.
- Maxing the track converts overflow reputation directly to doubloons.

#### Watch the Graveyards
- After each voyage, scan opponents' played characters.
- If someone already burned their strongest daytime-ability character, you can play more aggressively without fear of retaliation.
- Graveyards are public for a reason — use them.

#### Common Mistakes
- **Always playing your highest card** — Predictable and wasteful on mediocre loot days.
- **Ignoring relics** — Sometimes grabbing a relic to **deny** an opponent who benefits from curses is correct.
- **Ignoring reputation income** — Reputation is not just a tiebreaker; it pays doubloons every voyage.
- **Not planning across voyages** — Characters saved in Voyage 1 combine with new cards in Voyage 2. Think ahead.
- **Forgetting anchor abilities** — Characters in your ship trigger anchor effects at voyage end. Some are game-swinging.
- **Playing scared** — Sometimes the right move is a low-rank character specifically to grab a relic before an opponent who would benefit from it.

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Your First Game of Libertalia

**Setup**: Everyone receives the same 6 character cards. Each card shows a rank number, a name, and abilities with timing icons (sun = daytime, sunset = dusk, moon = night, anchor = end-of-voyage). The island board displays rows of face-up loot tokens (one row per day, tokens equal to player count per row).

**Voyage 1, Day 1**
*Step 1*: Look at the loot tokens in Day 1's row. Identify the most valuable pieces and ones to avoid.
*Step 2*: Choose a character that fits:
- Excellent loot available? Play a **high-rank** character for first pick at dusk.
- Mediocre loot or relics? Play a **low-rank** character with a strong daytime ability.
*Step 3*: Place chosen character face-down. Reveal simultaneously.
*Step 4*: Resolve daytime abilities (low rank to high), collect loot (high rank to low), resolve night abilities.

**Days 2-4**
- Repeat the same process each day. Your hand shrinks as you play cards.
- By Day 4, you have 3 cards left — play 1, keep 2 for next voyage.

**End of Voyage 1**
1. Resolve **anchor abilities** on characters in your ship.
2. Resolve **loot effects** — Chests: +5 doubloons each. Maps: 7 per pair, 12 per set of 3. Relics: -3 each.
3. **Add doubloon total to score dial** (permanently locked in). Return physical tokens and loot to supply.
4. Move ship characters to graveyard.

**Voyage 2**
- Collect **reputation income** based on track position.
- Deal 6 new characters (same process — one player draws, everyone matches).
- Keep your 2 unplayed cards from Voyage 1, giving you **8 cards** total.
- This voyage lasts **5 days** (one longer than Voyage 1). New loot drawn for each row.

**Voyage 3**
- Collect reputation income again.
- Deal 6 more characters added to remaining hand (could be **9+ cards** by now).
- Lasts **6 days** — the longest voyage. Maximum flexibility and maximum deduction opportunity.
- Check everyone's graveyards to narrow down what they hold.

**Final Scoring**
- After Voyage 3's anchor abilities and final score dial update, most doubloons wins.
- For your first game, **30-50 doubloons** is a solid target depending on player count.

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Reputation Track Manipulation
- The track has only **6 spaces**, making every position shift meaningful.
- **Reputation controls:** tiebreakers when players pick the same character, and doubloon income at the start of each voyage.
- **Key insight** — Reputation swings are most valuable early in a voyage because more days remain for that tie-breaking advantage.
- Push reputation before big loot days, not after.
- **Overflow rule** — At the top of the track, additional reputation converts to doubloons. Characters that boost reputation are never wasted.

#### Hand Reading
- All players receive the same 6 characters each voyage; unplayed cards carry forward.
- **Track opponents via graveyards** — Every played character is visible face-up.
- By later days of a voyage, you can predict opponents' plays with increasing accuracy.
- **Strongest reads** come from knowing which characters someone **did not** play. Cross-reference graveyards with dealt characters to narrow options.
- By Voyage 3, graveyards are full of information.

#### Loot Valuation
- **Chests** — Flat **5 doubloons**. Always solid, never bad.
- **Maps** — **7 per pair, 12 per set of 3**. A single map is worth nothing, but pairs and triples are among the highest-value loot. Prioritize maps when you already have one.
- **Amulets** — Safe **3 doubloons**. Take when nothing better is available.
- **Relics** — Cost **3 doubloons**, but taking one to deny an opponent whose characters benefit from curses can be net-positive.
- **Sabers** — Discard an opponent's character. Devastating when timed to remove a character before its anchor ability triggers.
- **Hooks** — Preserve a character across voyages. Strongest paired with powerful anchor abilities.
- Always value loot based on your current collection and board state, not in isolation.

#### Day Order Manipulation
- **High rank** — Best loot pick (dusk: highest first) but daytime abilities resolve last (lowest first).
- **Low rank** — Daytime abilities resolve first (great for aggression) but last loot pick.
- Sometimes you **want** to play low even with decent loot — striking first with a daytime ability can remove an opponent's character before it collects loot.
- Think about which phase matters more on a given day.

#### Voyage Pacing
- Voyages grow longer: **4 days, then 5, then 6**.
- **Early days** — Build position: collect barrels for reputation, grab hooks to preserve anchor characters, play expendable low-rank characters.
- **Final days** — Save strongest characters for when stakes are highest and opponents have committed their best cards.
- In Voyage 3 (6 days), you have room to set up early and close strong. The last 2 days of the final voyage are often where games are won or lost.
